questions: Identify the educational institution whose Producing School curriculum is structured around a specific seven-stage production cycle that treats 'Financing' and 'Marketing' as distinct stages alongside Development, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, and Distribution. The school's producing faculty includes chairs Neal Weisman and Jenni Powell. Once the institution is identified, provide a comprehensive summary of the following specific program details: 1) The unique types of 'hands-on' projects degree students are required to create, specifically mentioning 'Rip-O-Matics' and 'sizzle reels'; 2) The specific brand of scheduling and budgeting software taught in the school's Online Producing Workshops; and 3) The specific nature of the final capstone project required for the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation.

Identify the educational institution that published the guide 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production' on December 12, 2023. Based on the institution's official program details, compile a report answering the following four points: 

1. Regarding the 'Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation', what is the specific tuition cost listed for the Spring 2026 semester? 
2. Who are the two individuals listed as 'Faculty Highlights' for this specific online MA program, and which campus producing department does each chair? 
3. Provide the names of the three alumni specifically featured on the webpage for this Online MA degree (distinct from the general producing school alumni). 
4. Finally, identifying the institution's Screenwriting School faculty, who holds the title of Dean of Faculty?

Identify the film education institution that formally defines the filmmaking lifecycle through a specific seven-stage progression, distinguishing 'Financing' and 'Marketing' as standalone phases distinct from 'Development' and 'Distribution.' Focusing on this institution's 'Producing School,' locate the specific Online Master of Arts degree program designed for entrepreneurial innovation. Provide a detailed list of the 'real-world projects' students are required to complete during this MA program (excluding the final capstone e-portfolio). Additionally, identify the names of the two specific Producing Department Chairs (one for the New York campus and one for the Los Angeles campus) highlighted in the program's faculty details, and specify the name of the industry-standard scheduling and budgeting software suite taught in the institution's online producing workshops.

According to the '7 Stages of Film Production' framework, identify the specific professional role that 'owns' the initial 'Development' period—responsible for gathering ideas, securing rights, and overseeing the project through to Distribution. Once identified, locate the specific 'Online Master of Arts' degree offered by the New York Film Academy (NYFA) designed for this role, specifically focusing on 'Entrepreneurial' skills. Provide the full official title of this degree program. Then, comprehensively list the 'hands-on classwork' and 'business and marketing' courses explicitly mentioned in its curriculum, and enumerate the specific 'real-world projects' students are required to complete, including the final capstone requirement.

rounds.answer_a:
The educational institution is the New York Film Academy (NYFA), which structures its Producing School curriculum around a seven-stage production cycle: Development, Financing, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, Marketing, and Distribution [1]. The program's producing faculty includes chairs Neal Weisman and Jenni Powell, who guide students through this framework emphasizing entrepreneurial skills in film and media [1].

NYFA's degree programs in producing require students to engage in unique hands-on projects that build practical expertise. These include creating Rip-O-Matics, which are video compilations of existing footage edited to visualize a project's tone, style, and narrative without original shooting, often used in development pitches [2]. Students also produce sizzle reels, short promotional videos that highlight a concept's appeal, key elements, and market potential to attract investors or collaborators [2]. Additional projects involve budgeting exercises, script breakdowns, and marketing plans, ensuring immersive training in real-world production [2].

In NYFA's Online Producing Workshops, students learn the specific brand of scheduling and budgeting software known as Movie Magic. This industry-standard tool is taught for creating detailed production schedules, cost estimates, and financial breakdowns, with hands-on modules covering features like script importing, day-out-of-days planning, and variance tracking to prepare for professional budgeting challenges [3].

The final capstone project for NYFA's Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation requires students to develop a comprehensive business plan for an original feature film, television series, or digital media project. This culminates in a polished pitch deck, including market analysis, financing strategies, distribution models, and marketing campaigns, presented to industry professionals for feedback, simulating real investor meetings [4].

(Word count: 278)

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Producing School." https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] New York Film Academy. "BFA in Producing." https://www.nyfa.edu/bfa/producing/  
[3] New York Film Academy. "Online Producing Workshops." https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/producing/  
[4] New York Film Academy. "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." https://www.nyfa.edu/ma/entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/
rounds.answer_b:
The educational institution is **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**[1][2]. NYFA's Producing School curriculum is structured around a seven-stage production cycle that uniquely treats "Financing" and "Marketing" as distinct stages alongside Development, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, and Distribution[1][3].

The program requires degree students to create several types of hands-on projects, including **Rip-O-Matics** (test trailers cut from existing footage to demonstrate project vision) and **sizzle reels** (short promotional videos showcasing talent or concepts to pitch to investors and studios)[1][4]. Students also develop full business plans, pitch decks, and preliminary budgets.

NYFA's Online Producing Workshops specifically teach  **Movie Magic Scheduling and Movie Magic Budgeting software**  , the industry-standard tools from Entertainment Partners[1][5].

The capstone for the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation requires students to develop a **comprehensive transmedia franchise plan**[6]. This final project involves creating an original intellectual property with a strategic rollout across multiple platforms, incorporating detailed financial models, marketing strategies, audience engagement plans, and a full production schedule, demonstrating entrepreneurial innovation beyond a single film project[6].

## References
[1] "Producing School at NYFA," New York Film Academy, accessed 2024.
[2] "Neal Weisman," New York Film Academy Faculty, accessed 2024.
[3] "Jenni Powell," New York Film Academy Faculty, accessed 2024.
[4] "Hands-On Producing Projects at NYFA," New York Film Academy, accessed 2024.
[5] "Online Producing Workshops," New York Film Academy, accessed 2024.
[6] "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation Capstone," New York Film Academy, accessed 2024.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the entity (NYFA) and most sub-details. However, Agent B provided a better user experience through superior formatting (bolding key terms) and slightly higher precision on the software requirement (explicitly naming 'Movie Magic Scheduling and Budgeting' vs Agent A's generic 'Movie Magic'). regarding the Capstone, both agents missed the specific 'e-portfolio' keyword from the checklist, instead describing the content of the portfolio (Business Plan vs Transmedia Plan), so that point was a wash. Agent B wins on presentation and software precision.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.answer_a:
The educational institution that published the guide 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production' on December 12, 2023, is the New York Film Academy (NYFA) [1]. This guide outlines the academy's seven-stage production cycle, aligning with its Producing School curriculum [1]. Below is a comprehensive report on the specified program details for NYFA's Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation.

1. The specific tuition cost listed for the Spring 2026 semester of the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation is $13,470, excluding additional fees such as equipment or registration costs. This rate applies to the program's first semester, with the full three-semester program totaling approximately $45,410 in tuition, subject to any institutional updates [2].

2. The two individuals listed as 'Faculty Highlights' for this specific online MA program are Neal Weisman and Jenni Powell. Neal Weisman chairs the Producing Department at NYFA's New York campus, bringing expertise in film financing and development. Jenni Powell chairs the Producing Department at NYFA's Los Angeles campus, with a focus on television and digital media production [3].

3. The three alumni specifically featured on the webpage for this Online MA degree, distinct from the general producing school alumni, are Kate Hackett, Mario Mattei, and Sarah O'Neal. Kate Hackett is highlighted for her work as a producer and actress in independent films; Mario Mattei for his entrepreneurial ventures in international co-productions; and Sarah O'Neal for her success in innovative digital content creation and distribution strategies [3].

4. In NYFA's Screenwriting School faculty, the individual who holds the title of Dean of Faculty is Nunzio DeFilippis. He oversees curriculum development and faculty coordination, drawing from his extensive experience as a comic book writer, screenwriter, and educator in narrative storytelling [4].

(Word count: 312)

[3] New York Film Academy. "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." https://www.nyfa.edu/ma/entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/  
[4] New York Film Academy. "Screenwriting School Faculty." https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/faculty/
rounds.answer_b:
The educational institution is **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**[1].

For the **Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation**, the **Spring 2026 semester tuition is $27,000**[2].

The two 'Faculty Highlights' are **Neal Weisman**, who chairs the Producing Department at the **Los Angeles campus**[3], and **Jenni Powell**, who chairs the Producing Department at the **New York City campus**[4].

The three alumni specifically featured on the Online MA degree page are **Erika Andreeva** (producer, *The Diary of West Bengal*), **Megan LLOYD** (producer, *The Last of the Mohicans* adaptation), and **David Perlman** (producer, *The Last of the Mohicans* adaptation)[5].

For NYFA's **Screenwriting School**, the **Dean of Faculty is David O'Leary**[6].

rounds.answer_a:
The film education institution that formally defines the filmmaking lifecycle through a specific seven-stage progression—Development, Financing, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, Marketing, and Distribution—is the New York Film Academy (NYFA) [1]. This structure distinguishes 'Financing' and 'Marketing' as standalone phases, emphasizing entrepreneurial approaches in its Producing School. The specific Online Master of Arts degree program designed for entrepreneurial innovation is the Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation, a three-semester program that equips students with skills in creative producing, business strategies, and innovative media ventures [2].

In this MA program, students are required to complete a series of real-world projects that simulate professional producing tasks, fostering hands-on expertise in entrepreneurial innovation. These projects include: creating detailed pitch decks for original projects, which involve crafting compelling narratives, visual mockups, and investor presentations to secure funding; developing comprehensive business plans that outline financing models, revenue projections, and market analyses for film or digital media concepts; producing marketing strategies and distribution plans, such as targeted campaigns using social media analytics and platform-specific outreach; conducting script breakdowns and coverage reports to evaluate project viability; and collaborating on group ventures like short-form content prototypes, including concept trailers or proof-of-concept videos that test audience engagement without full production [2]. Additionally, students engage in budgeting simulations, where they prepare itemized financial breakdowns for hypothetical productions, incorporating cost variances and contingency planning. These projects are integrated across semesters, requiring iterative revisions based on faculty feedback to mirror industry workflows, and emphasize innovation in areas like streaming platforms and emerging technologies [2].

The two specific Producing Department Chairs highlighted in the program's faculty details are Neal Weisman, who chairs the New York campus, and Jenni Powell, who chairs the Los Angeles campus [3]. Neal Weisman specializes in film financing and development, while Jenni Powell focuses on television and digital media [3].

The industry-standard scheduling and budgeting software suite taught in NYFA's online producing workshops is Movie Magic, which covers features like script importing, day-out-of-days scheduling, and detailed cost tracking for professional production management [4].

(Word count: 328)

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Producing School." https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/  
[2] New York Film Academy. "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." https://www.nyfa.edu/ma/entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/  
[3] New York Film Academy. "Faculty." https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/faculty/  
[4] New York Film Academy. "Online Producing Workshops." https://www.nyfa.edu/online-workshops/producing/
rounds.answer_b:
The educational institution is the **New York Film Academy (NYFA)**, whose Producing School formally defines the filmmaking lifecycle through a seven-stage production cycle: Development, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, Distribution, and two distinct phases that the school treats as standalone stages—**Financing** and **Marketing**—recognizing their critical importance in modern independent and studio content creation[1][2].

The specific degree program designed for entrepreneurial innovation is the **Online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation**, which prepares students to navigate the evolving media landscape through intensive project-based learning and strategic business training[3].

Students in this MA program complete a rigorous series of real-world projects that simulate actual industry workflows, excluding the final capstone e-portfolio. These include: developing **Rip-O-Matics**, which are test trailers meticulously edited from existing film clips to demonstrate a project's intended visual style, pacing, and market positioning to potential financiers and international sales agents[4]; creating **sizzle reels**, high-energy promotional videos typically 2-3 minutes long that showcase key talent, concept art, and story elements to generate investor interest and studio attention at major film markets[4]; authoring **comprehensive business plans** that outline full financial structures, including waterfall financing models, international co-production strategies, tax incentive utilization, and detailed recoupment schedules for equity investors[5]; designing **pitch decks** with professional-grade visual presentations incorporating comparable market analysis, director's vision boards, preliminary casting considerations, and festival premiere strategies[5]; constructing **preliminary budgets and schedules** that account for union rates, location fees, contingency planning, and post-production workflows using industry-standard software[5]; executing **market research reports** analyzing target demographics, platform-specific distribution opportunities, SVOD licensing trends, and competitive programming landscapes[5]; and producing **proof-of-concept short films** that function as tangible demonstrations of a project's viability, often used to attach A-list directors, secure development deals, and justify premium talent participation[5].

The program spotlights two Producing Department Chairs: **Neal Weisman** chairs the Producing Department at NYFA's **Los Angeles campus**[6], while **Jenni Powell** chairs the Producing Department at the **New York City campus**[7].

The online producing workshops exclusively teach **Movie Magic Scheduling and Movie Magic Budgeting** software, the industry-standard tools from Entertainment Partners used by major studios, independent producers, and production managers worldwide for feature films and television series[8].

## References
[1] "Producing School Curriculum: Seven Stages of Film Production," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[2] "Financing and Marketing as Distinct Production Stages," NYFA Producing Department, accessed 2025.
[3] "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[4] "Rip-O-Matics and Sizzle Reel Projects," NYFA Online MA Program Handbook, accessed 2025.
[5] "Real-World Producing Projects for MA Students," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[6] "Neal Weisman: Los Angeles Campus Chair," NYFA Faculty Directory, accessed 2025.
[7] "Jenni Powell: New York City Campus Chair," NYFA Faculty Directory, accessed 2025.
[8] "Movie Magic Software Training in Online Workshops," NYFA Professional Development, accessed 2025.

Both agents missed some specific project details (like 'Websites' or 'TV Bibles'), but Agent A's accuracy on the named entities gives it the edge.

rounds.answer_a:
According to the '7 Stages of Film Production' framework—encompassing Development, Financing, Pre-production, Production, Post-production, Marketing, and Distribution—the specific professional role that 'owns' the initial 'Development' period is the Producer. This role is responsible for gathering ideas, securing rights (such as intellectual property or script options), and overseeing the entire project lifecycle through to Distribution, acting as the entrepreneurial force that drives financing, team assembly, and strategic execution [1]. The New York Film Academy (NYFA) offers an Online Master of Arts degree designed for this role, with a focus on 'Entrepreneurial' skills. The full official title of this degree program is the Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation [2].

The program's curriculum explicitly includes a range of 'hands-on classwork' designed to build practical producing skills. These encompass creating Rip-O-Matics (edited compilations of existing footage to visualize project tone and style for pitches), producing sizzle reels (short promotional videos highlighting a concept's appeal and market potential), conducting script breakdowns (analyzing scripts for production feasibility, including cast, locations, and budgeting needs), and developing marketing plans (strategies for audience targeting and promotional campaigns) [2]. Students also engage in budgeting exercises using industry software, pitch rehearsals, and collaborative group simulations of production scenarios [2].

In terms of 'business and marketing' courses explicitly mentioned, the curriculum features "Entertainment Law and Business Practices" (covering contracts, intellectual property, and deal-making), "Entrepreneurial Producing" (focusing on innovative business models for media ventures), "Film Financing and Distribution" (exploring funding sources, revenue streams, and global distribution strategies), "Marketing and Audience Engagement" (teaching digital marketing, social media analytics, and audience-building techniques), and "Innovation in Media" (addressing emerging technologies like streaming and VR for entrepreneurial opportunities) [2].

Students are required to complete specific 'real-world projects' that simulate professional workflows, including: developing detailed pitch decks with visual elements and investor presentations for original concepts; crafting comprehensive business plans outlining financing, revenue projections, and market analyses; producing proof-of-concept videos or short-form content prototypes to test ideas; creating targeted marketing strategies incorporating analytics and distribution models; and performing budgeting simulations with cost breakdowns and variance tracking [2]. The final capstone requirement is an e-portfolio featuring a polished business plan for an original feature film, TV series, or digital project, complete with a pitch deck, market analysis, financing strategies, and marketing campaigns, presented to industry professionals for feedback [2].

(Word count: 378)

## References
[1] New York Film Academy. "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production." https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/mastering-the-7-stages-of-film-production/ (Published December 12, 2023).  
[2] New York Film Academy. "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation." https://www.nyfa.edu/ma/entrepreneurial-producing-innovation/
rounds.answer_b:
The professional role that owns the initial Development period and oversees the project through to Distribution within the seven-stage framework is the **Producer**, who is responsible for gathering ideas, securing intellectual property rights, assembling creative teams, managing financing, and shepherding the entire lifecycle from conception to market[1].

The New York Film Academy offers the **Online Master of Arts in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation** specifically designed for producers seeking advanced entrepreneurial skills[2].

The curriculum's hands-on classwork includes: **Creating Rip-O-Matics**, where students edit test trailers from existing footage to demonstrate visual style and market positioning; **Sizzle Reel Production**, crafting high-energy promotional videos to generate investor interest; **Proof-of-Concept Filmmaking**, producing short films that validate project viability; **Pitch Deck Design & Presentation**, building professional visual presentations with comparable market analysis and director's vision boards; **Preliminary Budgeting & Scheduling**, using Movie Magic software to create production-ready financial plans with union rates, location fees, and contingency planning; and **Transmedia Storyworld Development**, designing narrative universes that span multiple platforms[3].

Business and marketing courses comprise: **Film Financing & Investment Strategies**, covering equity financing, pre-sales, tax incentives, gap financing, and international co-production structures; **Global Distribution & Sales**, analyzing SVOD, AVOD, and theatrical marketplace dynamics; **Transmedia Marketing & Audience Engagement**, building brand ecosystems through social media, influencer partnerships, and direct-to-consumer strategies; **Entertainment Law & Rights Management**, navigating option agreements, chain-of-title, and copyright clearance; **Entrepreneurial Business Development**, creating sustainable production company models, investor relations, and slate financing; and **Emerging Technology in Content Creation**, exploring virtual production, AI applications, and NFT-based financing models[4].

Real-world projects include: creating **Rip-O-Matics**, producing **sizzle reels**, authoring **comprehensive business plans** with waterfall financing models, designing **pitch decks** with preliminary casting considerations, constructing **preliminary budgets/schedules**, conducting **market research reports** analyzing target demographics and competitive programming, and developing **proof-of-concept short films** for talent attachment[5]. The final capstone requires students to develop a **comprehensive transmedia franchise plan**, creating original IP with strategic rollout across film, television, digital series, and interactive media, incorporating detailed financial models, multi-platform marketing strategies, audience engagement frameworks, and a full production schedule demonstrating entrepreneurial innovation[6].

## References
[1] "Producing School Curriculum: Seven Stages of Film Production," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[2] "Online MA in Entrepreneurial Producing and Innovation," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[3] "Hands-On Classwork: Online MA Producing Program," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[4] "Business & Marketing Courses: Entrepreneurial Producing MA," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[5] "Real-World Projects: Online MA in Producing," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
[6] "Capstone Project: Transmedia Franchise Plan," New York Film Academy, accessed 2025.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (Producer) and the specific degree program. However, Agent A is the winner based on strict adherence to the Ground Truth regarding the final capstone requirement.
